title: The Log: What every software engineer should know about real-time data's unifying abstraction
author: Jay Kreps
note: The Kafka co-creator's classic essay framing the append-only, totally-ordered log as the unifying abstraction behind data integration, replication, and stream processing.

title: Apache Kafka Documentation: Design
author: Apache Kafka project
note: Official design rationale for Kafka's log-based broker: filesystem persistence, the consumer pull model, single-integer offsets, and at-most/at-least/exactly-once delivery semantics.

title: Streaming 101: The world beyond batch
author: Tyler Akidau
note: Freely readable foundation on unbounded data processing, including the crucial distinction between event time and processing time and why their skew matters.

title: Streaming 102: The world beyond batch
author: Tyler Akidau
note: The sequel digs into watermarks as a notion of input completeness, windowing strategies, triggers, and handling late (straggler) data with accumulation modes.

title: Debezium Architecture
author: Debezium project
note: Official docs showing how CDC connectors tail database transaction logs (e.g. MySQL binlog, Postgres logical replication) and stream row-level changes into Kafka topics.

title: Turning the database inside out with Apache Samza
author: Martin Kleppmann
note: Kleppmann's Strange Loop 2014 talk reimagining the database as an immutable event log with materialized views maintained by stream processors; page links video, slides, and transcript.

title: Timely Stream Processing (Apache Flink Documentation)
author: Apache Flink project
note: Flink's concepts page defines event time vs processing time and explains watermarks, lateness, and tumbling/sliding/session windows in a production stream processor.

title: Event Sourcing
author: Martin Fowler
note: Fowler's original pattern write-up: capture all state changes as a sequence of events, enabling complete rebuilds, temporal queries, and event replay.
